title: Peeking at the End
subtitle: Check the latest entries with tail

sections.instruction: `head` shows the beginning, but when something goes wrong with a server, the most recent events are at the end. `tail` shows the last 10 lines — the freshest data.
sections.prompt: Type `tail log.txt` to see the most recent log entries.

onSuccess.message: The last 10 entries! You can see recent activity: file uploads, search queries, cache operations, and a sales report being generated. When debugging a problem, the end of the log is where you look first.

sections.instruction: Just like `head`, you can control how many lines `tail` shows with `-n`.
sections.prompt: Type `tail -n 3 log.txt` to see only the 3 most recent entries.

onSuccess.message: The three latest events. In the real world, developers run `tail` on log files constantly to check what just happened — especially when something breaks.

sections.instruction: You can also use `tail` on data files. Let's grab the very last record from the CSV.
sections.prompt: Type `tail -n 1 data.csv` to see the last row of data.

onSuccess.message: Chris Evans, the most recently listed person in the dataset. Remember: `head -n 1` gives you the headers, `tail -n 1` gives you the last record. A handy pair.

completionMessage: You now have three ways to read files: `cat` for the whole thing, `head` for the beginning, `tail` for the end. Together, they let you quickly inspect any file without opening an editor.
